title: ADK-203: Stateful Conversation Memory
description: Implement conversation storage adapters for sliding windows, summarization, Redis caches, and persistent Postgres backends.

highlights.title: Memory Windows
highlights.description: Design conversational sliding windows that maintain recent context without exceeding token quotas.

highlights.title: Redis Cache
highlights.description: Setup fast memory caches that save session chats with custom TTL timeouts.

highlights.title: PostgreSQL Store
highlights.description: Build relational persistence models that store, fetch, and restore multi-user conversations.

modules.title: 1. Introduction to agent conversation memory paradigms

blocks.content: Conversation Memory Paradigms
LLMs are inherently stateless. Each request must pass the historical message context. Managing this context limits token overhead.

items.title: Full History
items.content: Sends every message. Accurate but runs out of context tokens quickly.

items.title: Sliding Window
items.content: Only keeps the last N messages, discarding older context.

items.title: Summarization
items.content: Summarizes older history into a single paragraph to save tokens.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What is the primary drawback of sending full conversation history with every message?
- A) It is slow to parse on the client.
- B) It exhausts token limits, increasing costs and response latencies.
- C) It requires database write locks.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Full history consumes tokens rapidly.
Windowed Memory vs Summarized Memory
Sliding windows are simple and maintain precise local context. Summarized memory uses an intermediate LLM run to condense historical turns, preserving older context at lower accuracy.
Window Configuration Example

blocks.code:
from google_adk.memory import WindowedMemory

# Configure memory to store only the last 4 conversational turns
memory = WindowedMemory(window_size=4)

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: When is a sliding window memory format preferred over summarization?
- A) When long-term history is critical.
- B) When precise details of the last few messages must be preserved without distortion.
- C) When saving maximum tokens is the only priority.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Windows preserve exact wording for recent turns.
Redis Memory Persistence
Redis is an in-memory database ideal for low-latency session caches. In multi-user setups, storing thread history in Redis ensures horizontal scaling.
Python Redis Adapter Setup

blocks.code:
import redis
from google_adk.memory import RedisMemoryStore

r_client = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0)
# Connect the memory manager to Redis with a 1 hour TTL
store = RedisMemoryStore(client=r_client, ttl_seconds=3600)

blocks.title: TTL Strategy
blocks.content: Setting an explicit TTL ensures stale chat sessions are evicted, managing memory usage automatically.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: Why is Redis highly suitable for session memory stores?
- A) It supports complex SQL queries.
- B) It offers extremely low-latency reads and writes with automatic TTL eviction.
- C) It runs inside the client web browser.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Redis handles fast lookups and auto-eviction.
Multi-Tenant Memory Isolation
In a production environment, multiple users talk to the same backend. You must isolate memory threads to prevent data leaks.
Key Isolation Pattern

blocks.title: Data Security Warning
blocks.content: Never fall back to global shared memory variables. Any concurrent query will leak conversation history to other sessions.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: How do you prevent session leaks in multi-tenant configurations?
- A) Run a separate agent VM for every user.
- B) Partition chat memory keys using distinct namespaces (tenant:user:thread).
- C) Turn off all history persistence.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Namespace keys keep threads isolated.

modules.title: 5. Lab: Building a stateful memory management agent

blocks.content: Lab: Building a Stateful Memory Management Agent
In this lab, you will build a Python program that saves session histories to a local SQLite database, loading and resuming them dynamically.
Step 1: Write the SQLite Memory Manager

blocks.code:
import sqlite3
import json

class SQLiteMemoryStore:
    def __init__(self, db_path="chats.db"):
        self.conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
        self.conn.execute("CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS history (session_id TEXT, messages TEXT)")
        self.conn.commit()
        
    def save_session(self, session_id, messages):
        msg_str = json.dumps(messages)
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute("INSERT OR REPLACE INTO history (session_id, messages) VALUES (?, ?)", (session_id, msg_str))
        self.conn.commit()
        
    def load_session(self, session_id):
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute("SELECT messages FROM history WHERE session_id = ?", (session_id,))
        row = cursor.fetchone()
        return json.loads(row[0]) if row else []

if __name__ == '__main__':
    store = SQLiteMemoryStore()
    sample_msgs = [{"role": "user", "content": "Hi"}, {"role": "model", "content": "Hello!"}]
    store.save_session("sess_100", sample_msgs)
    print("Loaded Session:", store.load_session("sess_100"))

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What database is used in the script above to store the conversation messages?
- A) MongoDB
- B) PostgreSQL
- C) SQLite

Feedback: Option C is correct. SQLite is used to store and restore session history.
